summary: Jeff Atwood explores the surprising origins of common programming terms and conventions that developers take for granted. He traces 'foo' and 'bar' back to pre-WWII comic strips rather than the commonly assumed FUBAR military acronym. The word 'widget' originated from a 1924 satirical play about corporate meaninglessness. He also explains that pangrams like 'Pack my box with five dozen liquor jugs' were used by the Beagle Brothers for font sampling, demonstrating how programming culture absorbed terminology from unexpected sources in entertainment and business.
key_points: The word 'foo' predates FUBAR, originating in the Smokey Stover comic strip from 1930-1952, where it appeared as nonsense phrases like 'He who foos last foos best'
'Widget' comes from the 1924 play 'Beggar on Horseback' where it represented meaningless corporate products in a satirical nightmare sequence
Pangrams used in font displays have varied histories, with Beagle Brothers choosing one involving alcohol to match their homebrew spirit
Many programming conventions have roots in pre-computer culture from comics, theater, and business school terminology
money_quotes: When 'foo' is used in connection with 'bar' it has generally traced to the WWII-era Army slang acronym FUBAR, later modified to foobar. Early versions of the Jargon File interpreted this change as a post-war bowdlerization, but it now seems more likely that FUBAR was itself a derivative of 'foo'
The word 'foo' itself had an immediate prewar history in comic strips and cartoons
He who foos last foos best
Part of the point, of course, is that no one ever tells him what 'widgets' are
It's a pangram: it uses all 26 letters of the alphabet in a single sentence
The pangram Beagle Bros chose is a great expression of their homebrew spirit: it's rather clever and it involves alcohol
key_insight: Programming jargon that seems technical and modern often has whimsical origins in pre-computer entertainment and culture, from comic strips to satirical plays.
